title: Cells and Electrode Potential, Nernst Equation

concepts.title: What is Electrolysis
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrolysis

Electrolysis is a process in which chemical reactions occur at the electrodes, dipping in the electrolytes, when voltage is applied across them. The electrode that is charged positively is called anode and the electrode that is charged negatively is called cathode.

Let us two suitable metal electrodes, which are inserted in the electrolyte solution of $\text{MCl}$. The rods are now connected to a source of electromotive force (E.M.F.) As soon as the electrodes are connected to battery, the cations start moving towards cathode, take up electrons from the rod and get reduced to deposit as metal while anions $\left( \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} \right)$move towards anode and get oxidised to release $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ gas. Thus, at anode, electrons are released at the rod and go into the battery and anode becomes positive electrode. The electrons from the battery enter cathode, making it negatively charged. Thus, flow of electrons takes place from anode the cathode outside the cell and inside the cell, electrons indirectly flow from cathode to anode.

The electrode at which oxidation takes place is called anode and the electrode at which reduction takes place is called cathode .

The reactions at the two electrodes are :

At cathode $:{{\text{M}}^{+}}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to \text{M}$

At anode $:\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\to 1/2\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

Thus, electrolysis is a process of chemical decomposition of the electrolyte by the passage of electric current. It is carried out in a cell called electrolytic cell .

concepts.title: Conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Conductors

Any substance, which allows the electric current to pass through it, is called an electrical conductor. There are two types of conductors.

concepts.title: Metallic conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
1. Metallic conductors 

Substances, which allow the passage of electricity through them without undergoing any chemical change.

Example: $\text{Cu},\text{Ag},\text{Al}$ etc.

concepts.title: Electrolytic conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrolytic conductors 
Substances that allow the passage of electricity through their molten state or aqueous solutions and undergo chemical decomposition.

concepts.title: Difference between metallic and electrolytic conduction
concepts.conceptBody: Difference between metallic and electrolytic conduction

concepts.title: Types of Electrolytes
concepts.conceptBody:
Types of Electrolytes

There are three types of electrolytes: Strong, weak and non electrolytes.

1. Strong electrolytes 

A strong electrolyte is one which undergoes complete ionization when dissolved in water. The solution contains only the ions and not molecules.

Examples: $\text{HCl},\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}},{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{NaOH},\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}},\text{NaCl},\text{KCl},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COONa}$ etc.

$\text{HCl }\to {{\text{H}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

$\text{NaCl }\to \text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

2. Weak electrolytes 

A weak electrolyte is one which undergoes partial ionization or dissociation. Here, in solution the ions and the dissociated molecules will be in equilibrium with each other. When such a solution is diluted, the degree of ionization increases. It becomes complete at infinite dilution.

Examples:

$\text{HCOOH},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH},\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COON}{{\text{H}}_{4}},{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ etc.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}^{+}}$

3. Non-electrolytes 

A substance which doesn’t allow the electric current to pass through is called a non electrolyte. These substances have no ions. Therefore they do not ionize in water (Covalent compounds).

Examples:

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}\text{, toluene, sugar, urea, C}{{\text{H}}_{4}},{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}\text{ etc}\text{. }$

concepts.title: Factors Influencing Electrolytic Conduction
concepts.conceptBody:
3.3 Factors Influencing Electrolytic Conduction

1. The inter-ionic attraction 

It is the intersection between the ions of the solute at low concentrations. It is now much at low concentrations the inter-ionic cone is very less, but at high concentrations it is appreciable.

The negative ion is pulled to the negative pole because it is surrounded by positive charges and vice versa.

2. The solvation of ions 

This interaction is between the ions of the solute and the molecules of the solvent. Larger the interaction, greater is the solvation, lower will be the mobility of ions.

3. Viscosity of the medium 

Interaction between the solvent molecules. The solvent-solvent interaction also lowers the mobility. More the viscosity less is the mobility.

4. Temperature 

Ionic mobility increases with increase in temperature, thus the conductance increases.

Increase in temperature also decreases solute-solute, solute-solvent and solvent-solvent interaction.

concepts.title: First law
concepts.conceptBody:
Faraday's Laws

The quantitative relationship between the amount of electricity passed through a cell and the amount of substances discharged at the electrodes was systematised by Michael Faraday in the form of the following laws:

 First law

The amount of substance discharged (deposited or dissolved) at the electrode is proportional to the quantity of the electricity passing through the electrolyte.

Mathematically, $\left. \begin{matrix} \text{w}\propto \text{q} \\ \text{ w}\propto \text{I}.\text{t}(\text{q}=\text{I}.\text{t}) \\ \text{w}=\text{ z}\text{.I}\text{.t } \\ \end{matrix} \right\}$ … (i)

where $\text{w}$ is the weight of the substance discharged at an electrode in gram; $\text{q}$ is the charge in coulomb, $\text{t}$ is the time of flow of electricity in second, $\text{I}$ is the current in ampere and $\text{z}$ is a constant known as the Electrochemical Equivalent which is defined as the number of grams of the substance deposited or dissolved by one coulomb of electricity.

concepts.title: Second law
concepts.conceptBody:
Second law

When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different solutions, the amounts of different substances deposited or dissolved at the electrodes in different electrolytic cells are proportional to their equivalent weights and in an electrolytic cell, chemically equivalent amounts of substances are discharged at both the electrodes.

Interpretation of Faraday’s Second Law 

Let us now interpret the second law of Faraday in a simple manner.

1 electron reduces and deposits $1{{\text{M}}^{+}}$ion at an electrode (i.e., ${{\text{M}}^{+}}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to \text{M}$)

$\therefore \quad 1$ mole of electrons shall reduce and deposit 1 mole of ${{\text{M}}^{+}}$ions

If the ion has a valency of $n$,

$\text{n}$ mole of electrons shall reduce 1 mole of ${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}$ ions.

$\therefore \quad 1$ mole of electrons shall reduce $\frac{1}{\text{n}}$ mole of ${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}$ ions.

concepts.title: Electrochemical Equivalent and Equivalent Weight
concepts.conceptBody:
Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

Thus the essential content of Faraday’s second law is that 1 faraday, which corresponds to 1 mole of electrons, liberates 1 equivalent of matter. 

In redox reactions, the amount of the reactant, corresponding to 1 mole of electrons is thus its equivalent mass. 

Electrochemical Equivalent and Equivalent Weight 

The weight in gram of a substance liberated by 1 coulomb of electricity is called electrochemical equivalent whereas the weight in gram liberated by 96500 (or 1 faraday or 1 mole electricity) is called Gram Equivalent Weight of the substance.

From Faraday’s law, we can deduce the relationship between the electrochemical equivalent and equivalent weight.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{w}\propto \text{Q} & \text{ (Ist Law) } \\ \text{w}\propto \text{E} & \text{ (IInd Law) } \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{w}=\frac{\text{QE}}{\text{F}}\quad \left( \frac{1}{~\text{F}}=\frac{1}{96500} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\text{w}}{\text{E}}=\frac{\text{Q}}{\text{F}}$

No. of gram equivalents = No. of Faradays of electricity

$\Rightarrow $ 1 gm eq = any substance $=1~\text{F}$ of electricity

Now there are two approaches to solve a problem

First calculate the number of faradays of electricity by using:

No. of faradays $=\frac{\text{Q}}{\text{F}}=\frac{\text{It}}{96500}$

(i) Now by using the definition:

$1\text{gm}$ eq. of any substance $\equiv 1~\text{F}$ of electricity passed

Calculate the number of gm. eq. and by using the definition of gm. eq. (gmeq. = mass/E), determine the amount of substance deposited.

(ii) Using anodic and cathodic reactions as follows:

Let us consider a typical anode reaction

${{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}}-\text{ne}\to \text{M}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{n}\left( {{\text{e}}^{\prime }}\text{s} \right)\equiv 1\text{ molecule of M}$

$\left. \Rightarrow \quad {{\text{N}}_{0}}(\text{n{e}'s} \right)\equiv {{\text{N}}_{0}}\text{ molecule of M}$ $\left( {{\text{N}}_{0}}\text{ : Avogadro number } \right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad {{\text{N}}_{0}}(\text{n{e}'s})\equiv 1\text{ mole of M}$

$\Rightarrow \quad nF\equiv 1\text{ }mole\text{ }of\text{ }M$ $\text{(charge of }{{\text{N}}_{0}}\text{ electrons }\equiv 1~\text{F}=96500\text{C ) }$

So in this approach, first write anodic and cathodic reactions and derive the mole Vs faraday relation.

(iii) Using the combined relation obtained from Ist and IInd Laws:

$\text{w }=\text{Z I t}$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{w }=\frac{\text{E I t }}{96500}$

concepts.title: Construction and Working
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrochemical Cell

An electrochemical cell is a system consisting of electrodes that dip into an electrolyte and in which a chemical reaction either uses or generates an electric current.

A voltaic or galvanic cell is an electrochemical cell in which a spontaneous reaction generates an electric current.

Construction and Working 

A zinc rod is dipped in $\text{ZnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution and a $\text{Cu}$ rod in $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution. The $\text{Zn}$ rod is externally connected to the copper rod through a rheostate, a galvanometer and a plug key.

The two aqueous solutions are interlinked through a salt bridge which is an inverted ‘ $u$ ‘ tube which is filled with saturated semi solid paste of Agar Agar saturated in $\text{KCl}$ or $\text{KN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ solution [Agar agar is a carbohydrate which forms a jelly like substance when dissolved in hot water]

Working 

When the circuit is completed, a deflection is observed in the $(\text{G})$ towards the zinc electrode indicating that the ${{\text{e}}^{-}}$are flowing from the $\text{Zn}$ electrode to $\text{Cu}$ electrode.

At the $\text{Zn}$ electrode, oxidation takes place.

$\text{Zn}\to \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\text{(oxidation) }$ …(1)

The ‘e’ removed or lost or retained by the metal move through the material of the electrode and reach the $\text{Cu}$ electrode at which they are accepted by $\text{Cu}$ ions of the solution to form neutral copper atoms.

$\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{+2}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to \text{Cu (reduction) }$ … (2)

In an electrochemical cell, each electrode constitutes one half of the cell and the reaction taking place at the electrode is called half-cell reaction. The overall cell reaction is obtained by adding the two half-cell reactions (1) and (2).

$\text{Zn}+\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{+2}}\to \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+\text{Cu (Overall reaction) }$

From this it is found that when $\text{Zn}$ is added to $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution, $\text{Zn}$ displaces $\text{Cu}$ from $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ with the liberation of heat. But in the electrochemical cell there is no direct contact between $\text{Zn}$ and $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$. Hence whatever the heat energy that would have been liberated appears in the form of electrical energy. Hence the electrochemical cell acts as a source of current although for a short interval.

The electrode at which oxidation takes place or the metal rod becomes negative charged is called negative electrode.

The electrode at which reduction takes place or the metal rod becomes positive charged is called positive electrode. Accordingly, in the above constructed electrochemical cell, zinc electrode acts as negative electrode while $\text{Cu}$ electrode acts as positive.

concepts.title: Standard hydrogen electrode
concepts.conceptBody:
The Standard Hydrogen Electrode

The standard hydrogen electrode looks like this:

What is happening? 

As the hydrogen gas flows over the porous platinum, an equilibrium is set up between hydrogen molecules and hydrogen ions in solution. The reaction is catalysed by the platinum.

$2{{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{2(~\text{g})}}$

This is the equilibrium that we are going to compare all the others with.

Using the standard hydrogen electrode 

The standard hydrogen electrode is attached to the electrode system you are investigating-for example, a piece of magnesium in a solution containing magnesium ions.

Cells and half cells 

The whole of this set-up is described as a cell. It is a simple system which generates a voltage. Each of the two beakers and their contents are described as half cells.

The salt bridge 

The salt bridge is included to complete the electrical circuit but without introducing any more bits of metal into the system. It is just a glass tube filled with an electrolyte like potassium nitrate solution. The ends are “stoppered” by bits of control wool. This stops too much mixing of the contents of the salt bridge with the contents of the two beakers.

The electrolyte in the salt bridge is chosen so that it doesn’t react with the contents of either beaker.

What happens? 

These two equilibria are set up on the two electrodes (the magnesium and the porous platinum):

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{M}{{\text{g}}_{(\text{s})}}$

$2{{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{2(~\text{g})}}$

Magnesium has a much greater tendency to form its ions than hydrogen does. The position of the magnesium equilibrium will be well to the left of that of the hydrogen equilibrium

That means that there will be a much greater build-up of electrons on the piece of magnesium that on the platinum. Stripping all the rest of the diagram out, apart from the essential bits:

There is a major difference between the charge on the two electrodes - a potential difference which can be measured with a voltmeter. The voltage measured would be 2.37 volts and the voltmeter would show the magnesium as the negative electrode and the hydrogen electrode as being positive.

This sometimes confuses people! Obviously, the platinum in the hydrogen electrode isn’t positive in real terms - there is a slight excess of electrons built up on it. But voltmeters doesn’t deal in absolute terms - they simply measure a difference.

The magnesium has the greater amount of negativeness - the voltmeter records that as negative. The platinum of the hydrogen electrode isn’t as negative - it is relatively more positive. The voltmeter records it as positive.

What if you replace the magnesium half cell by a copper one? 

This means replacing the magnesium half cell by one with a piece of copper suspended in a solution containing $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ ions with a concentration of $1~\text{mold}{{\text{m}}^{-3}}$. You would probably choose to use copper(II) sulphate solution.

Copper forms its ions less readily than hydrogen does. Of the two equilibria:

$\text{Cu}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{(\text{s})}}\text{ }$

\[2{{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{2(~\text{g})}}\]

...the hydrogen one lies further to the left. That means that there will be less build up of electrons on the copper than there is on the platinum of the hydrogen electrode.

There is less difference between the electrical charges on the two electrodes, so the voltage measured will be less. This time it is only 0.34 volts.

The other major change is that this time the copper is the more positive (less negative) electrode. The voltmeter will show the hydrogen electrode as the negative one and the copper electrode as positive.

The emf of a cell measured under standard conditions is given the symbol $\text{E}$ cell

concepts.title: Cell conventions
concepts.conceptBody:
Cell Conventions

A quick way of drawing a cell

Drawing a full diagram to represent a cell takes too long. Instead, the cell in which a magnesium electrode is coupled to a hydrogen electrode is represented like this:

You will often find variants on the way the hydrogen electrode is represented, such as :

Cell Notation of An Electrochemical Cell

(i) Anode is written on the left side and cathode is written on the right side.

(ii) Phase boundaries are indicated by vertical bar or slash.

(iii) Concentration of the electrolytes in the anode and cathode must be written in parenthesis.

(iv) In case of a gas, the partial pressure is to be mentioned in atm or $\text{mmHg}$.

(v) A comma is used to separate two chemical species present in the same solution.

(vi) A double vertical line i.e. II denotes that a salt bridge is present.

(vii) EMF of the cell is written on the extreme right of the representation.

For example:

(i) $\text{Zn}(\text{s})\left| \text{ZnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\left( {{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{M} \right) \right|\left| \text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\left( {{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{M} \right) \right|\text{Cu}(\text{s})\quad ;{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$

(ii) $\text{Pt}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{P}}_{1}}\text{ atm } \right)|\text{HCl}(\text{cM})\text{AgCl}(\text{s})|\text{Ag}\quad \text{; E}_{\text{cell }}^{\prime }$

(iii) $\text{Pt}\left| \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}\left( {{c}_{1}}\text{M} \right),\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}\left( {{c}_{2}}\text{M} \right) \right|\left| \text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}(\mathbf{c}\text{M}) \right|\text{Ag}\quad ;{{\text{E}}^{\prime \prime }}\text{ cell }$

Note: In some cell representations (as in (ii) above), the salt bridge is not indicated which implies that the electrolyte is common to both anode and cathode compartments.

 

In the copper case :

concepts.title: Standard electrode potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Summarizing what standard electrode potentials tell

Remember that the standard electrode potential of a metal/metal ion combination is the emf measured when that metal/metal ion electrode is coupled to a hydrogen electrode under standard conditions.

What you are doing is comparing the position of the metal/metal ion equilibrium with the equilibrium involving hydrogen.

Here are a few typical standard electrode potentials:

Remember that each of these is comparing the position of the metal/metal ion equilibrium with the equilibrium involving hydrogen.

Here are the five equilibria (including the hydrogen one):

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{M}{{\text{g}}_{(\text{s})}}$

$\text{Zn}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{Z}{{\text{n}}_{(\text{s})}}$

$2{{\text{H}}^{+}}_{(\text{aq})}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{2(~\text{g})}}$

$\text{Cu}_{(\text{aq})}^{2+}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{u}}_{(\text{s})}}$

$\text{Ag}_{(\text{aq})}^{+}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\rightleftharpoons \text{A}{{\text{g}}_{(\text{s})}}$

If you compare these with the $\text{E}$ values, you can see that the ones whose positions of equilibrium lie furthest to the left have the most negative $\text{E}$ values. That is because they form ions more readily - and leave more electrons behind on the metal, making it more negative.

Those which don’t shed electrons as readily have positions of equilibrium further to the right. Their E values get progressively more positive.

concepts.title: Single Convention (IUPAC)
concepts.conceptBody:
Single Convention (IUPAC)

The reduction potential of a half cell is given a positive sign when the half cell reaction involves reduction, when coupled with a standard hydrogen electrode; and a negative sign when the half cell reaction involves oxidation, when connected with a standard hydrogen electrode.

concepts.title: Standard Half Cell Potential (Standard Electrode Potential) and Electrochemical Series
concepts.conceptBody:
Standard Half Cell Potential (Standard Electrode Potentlal) and Electrochemical Series

It will be discussed a little later that the half cell potential at a temperature depends upon the concentration of ions of the dissipated material. If for the half cell, $M\quad \mid {{M}^{\text{n}+}}(\text{aq}),\left[ {{\text{M}}^{\text{n}+}} \right]=1\text{M}$ at 25 potential is termed standard half cell potential or standard electrode potential.

Standard half cell potential, like half cell potential is also measured on standard hydrogen electrode scale.

Such a list of $E$ values of various half cells arranged in the given orders is known as electrochemical series.

concepts.title: Standard Reduction Potentials at 298 K
concepts.conceptBody:
Reduction Potential:

Some of the half cells or electrodes arranged in decreasing their E (reduction) values are as follows:

Standard Reduction Potentials at $\mathbf{298~K}$

$\begin{array}{ll} \hline \textbf{Reduction} & \textbf{Standard reduction potential} & \textbf{Reduction half} & \textbf{Standard reduction} \\ \hline \textbf{half reaction} & \textbf{E (in volts)} & \textbf{reduction} & \textbf{potential E (in volts)} \\ \hline L{{i}^{+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to Li & -3.05 & S{{n}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Sn & -0.14 \\ \hline {{K}^{+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to K & -2.93 & P{{b}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Pb & -0.13 \\ \hline B{{a}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Ba & -2.90 & 2{{H}^{+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to {{H}_{2}} & 0.00 \\ \hline C{{a}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Ca& -2.87& S{{n}^{4+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to S{{n}^{2+}}& 0.013 \\ \hline N{{a}^{+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to Na & -2.71 & C{{u}^{2+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to C{{u}^{+}} & 0.15 \\ \hline M{{g}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Mg & -2.37 & C{{u}^{2+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to C{{u}^{+}} & 0.15 \\ \hline A{{l}^{3+}}+3{{e}^{-}}\to Al & -1.66 & {{I}_{2}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to 2{{I}^{-}} & 0.53 \\ \hline M{{n}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Mn & -1.18 & F{{e}^{3+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to F{{e}^{2+}} & 0.77 \\ \hline 2{{H}_{2}}O\,(l)+2{{e}^{-}}\to {{H}_{2}}(g) 2{{H}^{-}}(aq) & -0.83 & A{{g}^{+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to Ag & 0.80 \\ \hline Z{{n}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Zn & -0.76 & B{{r}_{2}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to 2B{{r}^{-}} & 1.08 \\ \hline C{{r}^{3+}}+3{{e}^{-}}\to Cr & -0.74 & C{{l}_{2}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to 2C{{l}^{-}} & 1.36 \\ \hline F{{e}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Fe & -0.44 & {{O}_{2}}(g)+4{{H}^{+}}(aq)+4{{e}^{-}}\to \,2{{H}_{2}}O & 1.23 \\ \hline C{{d}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Cd & -0.40 & A{{u}^{3+}}+3{{e}^{-}}\to Au & 1.50 \\ \hline C{{o}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Co & -0.28 & C{{o}^{3+}}+{{e}^{-}}\to C{{o}^{2+}} & 1.82 \\ \hline N{{i}^{2+}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to Ni & -0.25 & {{F}_{2}}+2{{e}^{-}}\to 2{{F}^{-}} & 2.87 \\ \end{array}$

Note: 

(i) The standard half cell potential $\text{E}$ is an intensive property, like temperature or molar volume and so $\text{E}$ shall be the same for half cell reaction whether it is represented as $2{{\text{X}}^{+}}+2\text{e}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{X}}_{2}}$, or ${{\text{X}}^{+}}\text{e}\rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$. But unlike $\text{E},\Delta \text{G}$ (standard free energy change) is an extensive property which depends upon the mass, that is to say, if a half reaction, $2{{\text{X}}^{+}}+$ $2\text{e}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{X}}_{2}}$ is represented as ${{\text{X}}^{+}}+\text{e}\rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}{{\text{X}}_{2}},\Delta \text{G}$ of the latter would be half that of the former.

(ii) If the direction of a half cell (cell) reaction is reversed, its potential has the same magnitude but opposite sign, e.g., if for $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+2\text{e}\rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}$; $\text{E}=+0.34~\text{V}$ then for $\text{Cu}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+2\text{e};\text{E}=-0.34~\text{V}$

The same is also true for energy change.

(iii) Potentials are not thermodynamic functions and may not be added but the potential may be calculated from the free energy $\Delta \text{G}$, using $\Delta \text{G}=-\text{nFE}$. For example,

$\begin{align} & \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+\text{e}\to \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}};\text{E}=+0.77~\text{V};\quad \Delta \text{G}=-1(+0.77)\text{F}=-0.77~\text{F} \\ & \underline{\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}+2\text{e}\to \text{Fe};\text{E}=-0.44~\text{V};\quad \Delta \text{G}=-2(-0.44)\text{F}=+0.88~\text{F}} \\ & \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+3\text{e}\to \text{Fe}\quad \text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{On adding }:\Delta \text{G}=+0.11~\text{F} \\ \end{align}$

$\therefore \quad \text{E for }\left( \text{F}{{\text{e}}^{3+}}+3\text{e}\to \text{Fe} \right)=\frac{\Delta {{\text{G}}^{{}^\circ }}}{-\text{nF}}=\frac{0.11~\text{F}}{-3~\text{F}}=-0.04~\text{V}$

but not $(0.77-0.44)=0.33~\text{V}]$

concepts.title: Characteristics of electrochemical series
concepts.conceptBody:
Characteristics of Electrochemical Series

(1) Reactive metals are placed on top (e.g. Li) and they have a great tendency to get oxidized. Non-reactive metals like$\text{Ag}$ and $\text{Au}$.

(2) Any metal above hydrogen can displace it from dilute acids.

$\text{Example }:\text{Zn}+\text{ dil}\text{. }2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ZnC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

$\text{Cu}+\text{ dil}\text{. HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ no reaction}$

Any metal which is above another can displace that from its salt solutions.

Example: $\text{Zn}+\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ZnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{Cu}$

If two metals form a cell, the metal that is above undergoes oxidation and forms the anode while the one below forms the cathode.

$\text{ Example : Zn-Anode }\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ Cu-Cathode }$

$(0.76~\text{V})\xrightarrow{{}}(+0.34~\text{V})$

concepts.title: Calculation of Cell Potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Calculation of Cell Potential

In an electrochemical cell, electrons flow from negative electrode to the positive electrode. This shows that there is a potential difference between the two electrodes.

The minimum potential which causes the flow of the electrons from the negative electrode to the positive electrode is called emf.

The emf of a cell is defined as the algebraic difference between the SRP of the positive electrode and the SRP of the negative electrode even though oxidation is taking place at the negative electrode

Cell Potential $({{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}})={{\text{E}}_{\text{Cathode }}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{Anode }}}$!

In general, for a given electrode, the magnitude of oxidation and reduction potentials remain same but they differ with respect to their signs. Examples: For a cell made of a zinc electrode in $ZnS{{O}_{4}}$ and copper electrode in $\text{CuS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}\text{ }={{\text{E}}_{\text{Cu}+2/\text{Cu}}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{Zn}+2/\text{Zn}}}$

$=0.35-(-0.76)$

$=1.1~\text{V}$

concepts.title: Cell Potential and Nernst Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Cell Potential and Nernst Equation

Nernst equation is used to relate either half-cell potential or EMF of a cell with the concentration of the involved species. Let us first consider are dox change occurring in a electrochemical cell,

$\text{xA}+\text{yB}\rightleftharpoons \text{zC}+\text{aD}$

where A, B, C and D are the species whose concentrations vary i.e. they are either gases or solution phases. For species A, the free energy per mole of A can be given thermodynamically as

${{\text{G}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{G}}_{\text{A}}}+\text{RT}\operatorname{In}[\text{A}]$

For $x$ moles $A,{{x}_{A}}=x{{G}_{A}}+xRT\ln [A]=xGA+RT\ln {{[A]}^{x}}$ Similarly, for all other species,

$\text{y}{{\text{G}}_{\text{B}}}=\text{y}{{\text{G}}_{\text{B}}}+\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}$

$\text{z}{{\text{G}}_{\text{C}}}=\text{z}{{\text{G}}_{\text{C}}}+\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}$

and $\text{a}{{\text{G}}_{\text{D}}}=\text{a}{{\text{G}}_{\text{D}}}+\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}$

Now, the free energy change for the overall cell reaction can be deduced as

$\Delta \text{G}=\left( \text{z}{{\text{G}}_{\text{C}}}+\text{a}{{\text{G}}_{\text{D}}} \right)-\left( \text{x}{{\text{G}}_{\text{A}}}+\text{y}{{\text{G}}_{\text{B}}} \right)$

$=\text{z}{{\text{G}}_{\text{C}}}+\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}+\text{a}{{\text{G}}_{\text{D}}}+\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}-\text{xG}_{\text{A}}^{{}^\circ }-\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{A}]}^{\text{x}}}-\text{y}{{\text{G}}_{\text{B}}}-\text{RT}\ln {{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}$

$=\left( \text{z}{{\text{G}}_{\text{C}}}+\text{a}{{\text{G}}_{\text{D}}} \right)-\left( \text{x}{{\text{G}}_{\text{A}}}+\text{yG}{{\text{B}}_{\text{B}}} \right)+\text{RT}\ln \frac{{{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}{{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{x}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}}$

$\Delta \text{G}=\Delta {{\text{G}}^{{}^\circ }}+\text{RT}\ln \frac{{{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}{{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{x}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}}$ … (i)

where $\Delta \text{G}$ is the free energy change when all the reactants and products are present at one molar concentration.

Any spontaneous reaction occurring in a cell, occurs with a decrease in free energy. This decrease in free energy brings in an equivalent amount of electric work obtainable from a given system over and above any PdV energy that can be delivered to the surrounding. This can be calculated by the total charge driven through cell and the potential difference. Thus

$-\Delta \text{G }=\text{ Total charge EMF of the cell }$

$-\Delta \text{G }=\text{nF}\quad {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$

[Negative sign indicates decrease of free energy and it implies that as ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$ becomes more and more positive, $\Delta \text{G}$ will become more and more negative, making the reaction spontaneous] Similarly, $\quad -\Delta \text{G}=\text{nFE}$ cell

Therefore, equation (i) can be written as

$-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}+\text{RT}\ln \frac{{{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}{{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{x}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}}$

Dividing both the sides by \[-nF\] gives,

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell }}^{\text{o}}-\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{nF}}\ln \frac{{{[\text{C}]}^{\text{z}}}{{[\text{D}]}^{\text{a}}}}{{{[\text{A}]}^{\text{x}}}{{[\text{B}]}^{\text{y}}}}$

Putting $T=298~\text{K},\text{R}=8.314~\text{J}/\text{molK},\text{F}=96500\text{C,}$ we get

${{E}_{cell\text{ }}}=E_{cell\text{ }}^{o}-\frac{0.059}{n}\log \frac{{{[C]}^{z}}{{[D]}^{a}}}{{{[A]}^{x}}{{[B]}^{y}}}$ … (ii)

The equation (ii) is called Nernst equation, which is applicable to half-cell reactions as well as to complete cell reactions.

Daniel cell represented as$\text{Zn}(\text{s})\left| \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\left( {{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{M} \right) \right|\left| \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\left( {{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{M} \right) \right|$ assumes that $\text{Zn}$ is the anode and $\text{Cu}$ is the cathode. Such an assumption would be true only if the cell potential $\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}} \right)$ is positive.

The cell potential is given in the following three ways of which we would choose the third one in all our problems.

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Cathode })}}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{OP}(\text{ Anode })}}$

or ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{OP}(\text{ Anode })}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{OP}(\text{ Cathode })}}$

or ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Cathode })}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Anode })}}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Cathode) }}}$ is the reduction potential of the cathode while ${{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{Anode})}}$ is reduction potential of the anode. ${{\text{E}}_{\text{OP}(\text{ Cathode) }}}$ is the oxidation potential of the cathode while ${{\text{E}}_{\text{OP}(\text{ Anode) }}}$ is the oxidation potential of the anode.

Now, let us find the EMF of Daniel cell using Nernst equation. Since we need to represent the reduction potential of cathode and anode, we first need to write the relevant reduction reactions.

For cathode: $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Cu}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{nF}}\log {{\text{Q}}_{\text{c}}}\left( \operatorname{or}{{\text{Q}}_{\text{pc}}} \right)$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}}$ is the standard reduction potential of the given half reaction, $\text{R}$ is the universal gas constant, $\text{T}$ is the absolute temperature at which cell works, $\text{F}$ is the Faraday constant and $\text{n}$ is the number of mole of electrons as seen in the reaction. The expression in the log term should be that of ${{K}_{c}}$ or ${{K}_{pc}}$. This means that if reaction involves no gases, then the expression in the log term should be that of ${{K}_{c}}$ while if a gas is involved then the expression in the log term should be that of ${{K}_{pc}}$. In these expressions, the concentration should always be in moles per liter while the partial pressure should be in atmosphere units.

$\therefore \quad {{\text{E}}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

For anode: $\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Zn}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Zn}}}=\text{E}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Zn}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}$

As ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Zn}}}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}-\text{E}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Zn}}^{{}^\circ }+\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}_{\text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Zn}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell }}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

Note: Since ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$ has been defined as ${{\text{E}}_{\text{RP (Cathode) }}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Anode) }}}$, the Nernst expression holds good even if the number of mole of electrons of the two half reactions are different.

For example, consider the cell,

$\text{Pt}\left| {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right|\text{HCl}||\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}$

For cathode: $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Cu}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}}}\text{ICu}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

For cathode: ${{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}1/2{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

${{\text{E}}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}}=\text{E}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{1}\log \frac{{{\left[ {{\text{P}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}} \right]}^{1/2}}}{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{1}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}+\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\left[ {{\text{P}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}} \right]}^{1/2}}}{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}$

It is also possible to balance the electrons in both the half cell reactions and then subtract ${{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Anode) }}}$ from ${{\text{E}}_{\text{RP}(\text{ Cathode) }}}$. That is,

For anode: $2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

${{\text{E}}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}}=\text{E}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}}{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\mid \text{Cu}}^{{}^\circ }-\text{E}_{{{\text{H}}^{+}}\mid {{\text{H}}_{2}}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.059}{2}\log \frac{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{1/2}}}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]{{\text{P}}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}}$

concepts.title: Determination of Thermodynamic Data
concepts.conceptBody:
EMF-Measurements: Applications

Emf measurements have wide applications such as in the determination of $\text{ pH},{{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}},\Delta \text{H},\Delta \text{S etc}\text{. }$

 Determination of Thermodynamic Data:

  • $\Delta \text{G can be determined }$ 

$\Delta \text{G}=-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$ 

Using Gibbs – Helmholtz equation:

$\Delta \text{G}=\Delta \text{H}+\text{T}{{\left( \frac{\text{d}(\Delta \text{G})}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{p}}}$

$-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\Delta \text{H}-\text{nFT}{{\left( \frac{\text{d}\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}} \right)}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{p}}}$

concepts.title: Temprature coeffcient of the emf of the cell
concepts.conceptBody:
• Temp. coefficient of the emf of the cell can be determined

${{\left( \frac{\text{d}\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}} \right)}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{p}}}=\frac{\Delta \text{H}+\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}}{n\text{FT}}$

${{\left( \frac{\text{d}\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}} \right)}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{p}}}=\frac{\Delta \text{H}}{\text{nFT}}+\frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}}{\text{T}}$

concepts.title: Ethalpy change in a reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Enthalpy change of a reaction in cell

  • Enthalply change can be determined

$\Delta \text{H}=-n~\text{F}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}+\text{nFT}\left( \frac{\text{d}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}}{\text{dT}} \right)$
$=-nF\left[ {{E}_{cell\text{ }}}-T{{\left( \frac{d{{E}_{cell\text{ }}}}{dT} \right)}_{p}} \right]$

concepts.title: Entropy change in a reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
• Entropy change can be determined

$\Delta \text{G}=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}$

$\Delta S=-{{\left( \frac{d(\Delta G)}{dT} \right)}_{p}}$

$\Delta \text{S}=-{{\left( \frac{\text{d}}{\text{dT}}\left( -\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}} \right) \right)}_{\text{p}}}$

$\Delta \text{S}=\text{nF}{{\left( \frac{\text{d}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}}{\text{d}T} \right)}_{\text{p}}}$

$(a)$ $\Delta \text{H}=\text{nF}\left[ \text{T}\left( \frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}} \right)-\text{E} \right]\text{ where }$where $\frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}}$ is called temperature coefficient representing the change of EMF with the change of temperature, $n$ is the number of mole of electrons involved, $\text{F}$ is one Faraday, $\text{E}$ is EMF of the cell at temperature $\text{T}$ and $\text{T}$ is the absolute temperature. Depending upon the value of $\left( \frac{\text{d}E}{~\text{d}T} \right),\,\,\,\Delta \text{H}$ can be negative or positive i.e., reaction can be exhothermic or endothermic.

(b) $\Delta \text{S}=\text{nF}\left( \frac{\text{dE}}{\text{d}\mathbf{T}} \right)$. When $\left( \frac{\text{dE}}{\text{d}T} \right)$ is negative, the change in entropy would also be negative and when $\left( \frac{\text{dE}}{\text{dT}} \right)$ is positive change in entropy would be favoured, i.e., $\Delta \text{S}$ would be positive.

concepts.title: Determination of Equilibrium Constant
concepts.conceptBody:
Determination of Equilibrium Constant:

Let us assume that the redox change occurring in Daniel cell attains equilibrium. At equilibrium, the reduction potential values of the two electrodes become equal and EMF of cell becomes zero.

$\text{Zn}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}(\text{aq})\rightleftharpoons \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}(\text{aq})+\text{Cu}(\text{s})$

The equilibrium constant for this reaction is given as

$\text{Keq}=\frac{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

Apply Nernst equation to complete cell reaction

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell }}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{nF}}\ln \frac{\left[ \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

$\therefore $ $\text{E}_{\text{cell }}^{{}^\circ }=\frac{\text{RT}}{\text{nF}}\ln \text{Keq}\quad \left( \text{ as }{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=0 \right)$

$\therefore $ $\text{nF E cell }=\text{RT}\ln \text{Keq }$

$-\Delta \text{G}=\text{RT}\ln \text{Keq}$

$\Delta \text{G}=-2.303\text{RT}\log \text{Keq}$

Note: This relation is valid for many equilibrium constants like ${{\text{K}}_{\text{w}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{f}}},{{\text{K}}_{\text{d}}}$ etc.

concepts.title: Ionisation Constants of Weak Acid
concepts.conceptBody:
Ionisation Constants of Weak Acid

For such cases we determine $[{{\text{H}}^{+}}]$using a suitable reference half cell.

$\text{Pt}\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)\left| {{\text{H}}^{+}},\text{HA}\left( {{\text{C}}_{1}} \right) \right|\left| \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}\left( {{\text{C}}_{2}} \right) \right|\text{Cu}$

Its ${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$ is measured. Thus $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$in $\text{HA}$ can be determined

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.0591}{2}\log \frac{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}} \right]}$

If $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$is known, then by Ostwald’s dilution law 

$\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]=\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}}$

concepts.title: Determine pH of a Solution
concepts.conceptBody:
Determine $\mathbf{pH}$ of a Solution:

$\text{Pt}\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)\left| {{\text{H}}^{+}}(\text{pH}=\text{x}) \right|\left| \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}(1\text{M}) \right|\text{H}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{Hg}$

$\text{calomel}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} L.H.S.\text{ half cell} & {{H}_{2}}(g)\to 2{{H}^{+}}+2{{e}^{-}} & {{E}_{ox}}=0.00\,\,V \\ R.H.S.\,\text{ half cell} & H{{g}_{2}}C{{l}_{2}}(s)+2e\to 2Hg(l)+2C{{l}^{-}} & {{E}_{red}}=0.2676 \\ Net & \overline{{{H}_{2}}(g)+H{{g}_{2}}C{{l}_{2}}(s)\to 2{{H}^{+}}(aq)+2Hg(l)+2C{{l}^{-}}(aq)} & {{E}_{cell}}=0.2676\,\,V \\ \end{array}$

$\text{K}={{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} \right]}^{2}}{{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}$

$={{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}$ $\text{ (since }\left[ \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} \right]=1\text{M )}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell}}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell}}^{{}^\circ }-\frac{0.0591}{2}\log {{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}^{2}}=\text{E}_{\text{cell}}^{{}^\circ }+0.0591\text{pH}$

$\text{pH}=\frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}-{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}}{0.0591}$
